artifacts.content:
Hi Daniel,

Finally catching up on life admin and wanted to actually write instead of just sending you memes.

First, how are you holding up this semester? I keep thinking about that morning at Olympia Coffee in Tacoma back in May 2023 when you told me about the panic attack during the staff meeting – the one where they were going over the homelessness data and budget cuts, and you had to take that brief leave. I’m still angry that the district piled all of that on you with no real support. It’s been on my mind again because a couple of my HarborBridge projects are using similar student housing instability data, and I keep flashing back to that conversation with you at the window table.

On my end, things are…busy, in a familiar way. HarborBridge is good overall – it actually feels like the work matters – but I’m trying not to repeat the North Sound pattern of saying yes to everything. My therapist, Dr. Karen Liu over at Harborview Behavioral Health, has been pretty blunt about that. I’ve been seeing her since February 2023 for the stress-y insomnia and the whole early-burnout cocktail. We literally have MyChart session notes titled “work/volunteer boundary setting” and “processing secondary trauma from shelter homework club,” which is both on-the-nose and weirdly validating.

Between the Evergreen homework club kids’ stories and the datasets I stare at all day, my brain sometimes doesn’t know the difference between my own life and other people’s crises. Dr. Liu keeps reminding me that I’m not actually responsible for fixing the entire housing system.

Speaking of housing, you’ll appreciate this: in July, after we moved to Maple Terrace and got that cheerful notice about how much rent might jump at renewal in July 2025, I booked a 45‑minute meeting with a BECU financial planner, Eric Dawson, at the Rainier Valley branch. We went line by line through “what if we buy a tiny condo and pay for daycare at the same time?” and he basically said, very politely, that it would push us right up against a sketchy debt‑to‑income ratio unless one of us earns more or we seriously trim expenses.

So that’s been fueling my late‑night spreadsheet habit.

Anyway, I mostly wanted to say: I’m proud of you for staying in the classroom as long as it still feels right, but if you decide to walk away, that doesn’t make you a failure. Text me your next free weekend and I’ll drive down to Olympia; we can drag Dad out for coffee and I’ll bring you the housing policy book I keep ranting about.

Love,
Laura

Hi Mom and Dad,

Just got home and wanted to write while everything is still fresh instead of trying to recap over a rushed phone call.

Dad, I’m really glad you finally let us take you to Providence St. Peter after the fall last week. Watching you hobble around the living room pretending it was “just a tweak” was not convincing. Hearing the doctor in Olympia actually say the words “worsening chronic back issues” at least gives us something concrete to work with.

On the drive back to Seattle, I was thinking about how much you both already juggle, so I set up a shared Google Sheet called “Dad’s PT & meds” and invited Mom’s Gmail address. It has tabs for the physical therapy schedule, medications (with doses), and questions for the next appointment. Mom, I know you have your notebook system, but this way I can help from here – rescheduling PT if needed, calling the office, etc.

I also want to be honest that this is part of why I pushed so hard to leave North Sound when I did. Officially, I told them it was about aligning with education and housing nonprofits (which is true), but in a follow‑up email to my old manager I explained that after Dad’s fall and the St. Peter evaluation in November, I need enough flexibility to drive down to Olympia for PT appointments without getting side‑eyed for leaving “early.” HarborBridge is smaller and more mission‑driven, and so far they’ve actually meant it when they say “family first.”

Please don’t read this as me resenting anything. I want to be part of this. I just need us to be organized so it doesn’t all land on Mom by default.

Can we do a quick Zoom this Sunday afternoon to walk through the spreadsheet and the next few weeks of PT? I’ll also bring down my drill next visit so, Dad, you can supervise while I pretend to know what I’m doing with the loose railing.

Love you both,
Laura

Hi Maya,

You’re in the other room grading those advisory reflections, but if I say all of this out loud right now I’ll forget half of it, so: email.

I just got off the phone with Erin from Evergreen Family Shelter. She was lovely, as always, and asked (very gently) if I’d consider coming back for a regular weeknight homework club slot in the winter. I told her no, and I feel both relieved and guilty.

I explained that the new HarborBridge job has me on Zoom with clients until 5:30 or 6 some nights, and that I promised myself (and you) I wouldn’t go back to sprinting from work to a shift twice a week. I also mentioned that Wednesday evenings are now sacred because of our every‑other‑week sessions with Rachel Ortiz at Capitol Hill Therapy Collective. I didn’t go into detail, just said we’re using that time to work through long‑term planning and work–life boundaries. It still felt weird to say “our couples therapist” out loud to someone outside the inner circle, even though we’ve been seeing Rachel since August.

I also found myself telling Erin more than I expected about why I stepped down in June. I said that my therapist, Dr. Karen Liu at Harborview Behavioral Health, has been pretty clear that the stress‑related insomnia and early signs of burnout weren’t going to magically disappear if I kept stacking evening commitments on top of a full‑time analytics job. She even titled one of my MyChart notes “processing secondary trauma from shelter homework club,” which is exactly what it felt like some nights walking home.

Saying all of that, I realized how much the Columbia City house stuff is still in my body too. I didn’t tell Erin this part, but when we were talking about safe, stable housing for the kids, I flashed back to that March 2021 email I sent our old landlord – the one titled “Concerns about safety and repeated late-night outbursts,” after Mark pounded on the bedroom wall and yelled those slurs because he heard us talking. No wonder I care so much about kids having someplace they can exhale.

Anyway, thank you for holding that Wednesday space with me and for not rolling your eyes when I say no to things now. Let’s stick to the plan of stir‑fry and one episode of something mindless tonight.

Love,
L

Working title: “Why I left North Sound (for me only, not for anyone else).”

1. Official story is still true: I wanted work that aligns more directly with education and housing nonprofits. HarborBridge gives me that.

2. Unofficial but equally true: those last nine months at North Sound (Jan–Sep 2024) were a slow drip of ethical heartburn. I keep thinking about that March 2024 email from E. (“Can we smooth out the retention dip before the board sees this?”) and the Zoom where he screen‑shared my Tableau dashboard and tried to nudge me toward changing cohort definitions so the line wouldn’t look so jagged. I said no, but the pressure was there.

3. That’s why there’s a folder on my personal laptop called “NSDS – data integrity concerns.” It has redlined reports, email threads, and my own notes. I’ve never shown it to anyone. It exists because I needed to know I wasn’t imagining the gaslighting.

4. Layer on top of that the stuff outside work: seeing Dr. Karen Liu at Harborview Behavioral Health since February 2023 for stress‑related insomnia and early burnout signs; MyChart notes literally titled “work/volunteer boundary setting.” The nights I couldn’t fall asleep after homework club at Evergreen because I was replaying kids’ stories about bouncing between shelters.

5. Then Dad’s fall in early November 2024 and the trip to Providence St. Peter in Olympia, the doctor talking about “worsening chronic back issues,” me quietly setting up the “Dad’s PT & meds” Google Sheet so Mom wouldn’t have to carry it all. Realizing I need a job where leaving at 3 p.m. to drive south for a PT appointment doesn’t feel like an act of rebellion.

6. And the practical layer: that July 2024 meeting with Eric Dawson at the BECU Rainier Valley branch about whether we can afford both a condo and daycare someday without blowing our debt‑to‑income ratio. His face when he walked me through the numbers was…sobering.

All of these pieces matter. Remember this when you start wondering if you “overreacted” by leaving.

metadata.title: Why I really left North Sound

artifacts.content:
Event: Individual therapy – Dr. Karen Liu
Date: Thursday, 2024-03-14
Time: 5:30–6:20 p.m.
Location: Harborview Behavioral Health Clinic, 3rd floor

Description/Notes to self:

– This is session #6 with Dr. Liu since starting in Feb 2023. Goal today: be honest about how frayed I feel.

– Topics to cover:
  • Work: North Sound crunch, E. pushing on “smoothing” retention metrics again before the April board deck. Bring printout of that March email where he wrote, “Can we smooth out the retention dip before the board sees this?” and my draft response.
  • Volunteering: Evergreen Family Shelter homework club. Talk about why I still can’t shake the stories from the kids even after I get home. Ask if it’s normal that I wake up at 3 a.m. worrying about whether they’re still in the same room at the shelter.
  • Insomnia: track how many nights this week I’ve been up past 1 a.m. scrolling instead of reading. Mention the MyChart note she titled “processing secondary trauma from shelter homework club” and whether there’s anything more concrete I can do besides “better sleep hygiene.”

– Also want to briefly mention the October 2022 consult at UW Medicine Roosevelt Women’s Health Clinic about possible egg freezing. It’s still floating in the back of my mind. Maya and I keep saying we’ll revisit the whole “kids” question when things calm down, but between my schedule, her counseling caseload, and the rent at Maple Terrace, I don’t see a magical calm period appearing.

– Ask for ideas on boundaries so I don’t say yes to another weekly volunteer gig on top of everything just because someone needs help.

Subject: Trying to figure out timing (and bandwidth)

Hey Emily,

I owe you about 17 updates, but I’ll start with the big brain‑space one.

You remember that October 2022 appointment I had at UW Medicine’s Roosevelt Women’s Health Clinic to talk about fertility preservation? The one where the doctor walked me through egg‑freezing timelines and printed out the price ranges for one cycle and the meds, and I came home with a folder I promptly hid in the closet? That’s still very much in play.

Maya and I had another long talk after our Wednesday session with Rachel Ortiz at Capitol Hill Therapy Collective. We’ve been seeing her every other week since August to work through the “are we actually going to try for a baby?” question and all the work–life stuff around it. Rachel keeps gently asking what we’re willing to give up to make space, and I keep staring at my calendar like it’s going to rearrange itself.

Part of why I’m hesitating to jump back into anything like the twice‑weekly Evergreen homework club schedule is that I want some margin if we decide to move forward with fertility treatments in the next year or two. Between potential monitoring appointments at Roosevelt, my HarborBridge workload, and Maya’s school counselor hours, we’re already playing Tetris.

And then there’s the money. After that July 2024 meeting with Eric Dawson at the BECU Rainier Valley branch – where he basically showed me that adding daycare plus a mortgage on even a tiny condo would push us right up against a not‑great debt‑to‑income ratio – I’m very aware that an egg‑freezing cycle isn’t pocket change either.

All of this is layered on top of the usual Laura stew: worrying about my Evergreen kids even after stepping back in June, trying not to bring work trauma home (thank you, Dr. Liu at Harborview, for the “boundary setting” pep talks), and now helping coordinate Dad’s PT schedule after his St. Peter back evaluation.

Anyway, this is long, but you’re one of the few people I can dump the whole messy picture on. If you have bandwidth for a Zoom soon, I’d love to hear how you and Oliver navigated the “when” decision, even though your path is obviously different.

Love,
Laura

Thread: Laura & ex‑coworker (Signal)

[2024-09-18, 9:07 p.m.]
Jess: so how’s harborbridge? you look less dead in your IG stories lol

Laura: lol low bar but yeah, better

Jess: real talk – did you actually leave NSDS just for “mission alignment” or was there more tea 👀

Laura: short answer: both

Laura: longer answer (since this is encrypted): by the time I left in Sept I had a whole folder on my home laptop called “NSDS – data integrity concerns.”

Jess: oh wow

Laura: remember that March 2024 email from E.? “Can we smooth out the retention dip before the board sees this?” that one. plus the zoom where he tried to get me to redefine cohorts so the Tableau line looked nicer. I kept redlines + my notes bc I honestly started doubting my own memory.

Jess: jesus. glad you got out

Laura: same. but it took Dr. Liu basically spelling out “this is burning you out” before I believed myself.

Jess: therapist?

Laura: yeah, Karen Liu at Harborview Behavioral Health. been seeing her since Feb 2023 for stress‑insomnia + the whole early‑burnout thing. MyChart literally has a note titled “processing secondary trauma from shelter homework club.”

Jess: oof

Laura: add on top of that Dad’s fall last November – we spent half a day at Providence St. Peter in Olympia while they evaluated his back. I’m now the unofficial project manager for his PT + meds via a shared Google Sheet w/ my mom.

Jess: that’s a lot

Laura: and meanwhile Maya + I are in couples sessions w/ Rachel Ortiz at Capitol Hill Therapy Collective every other Wed trying to figure out work‑life + maybe‑kids. so yeah, “mission alignment” is true, but also: I needed out before something snapped.

Jess: proud of you for not setting yourself on fire for dashboards

Laura: lol that should be on a mug

Hi Daniel,

I hope you’re doing well! I wanted to catch up and share some updates, especially since I’ve had a few changes recently. As you know, since moving to Seattle in 2016, I’ve been deeply involved in volunteering with education and housing nonprofits. My most recent stint was with Evergreen Family Shelter from 2020 until this past June. I was supervising about four volunteers there, coordinating evening homework sessions for kids in transitional housing, but had to step back due to the new role at HarborBridge.

Before that, I was an Adult ESL Classroom Assistant at Rainier Valley Community Learning Center from early 2017 to March 2020. It was such a rewarding experience helping adult learners with English through practical exercises like drafting emails and job interview prep. Unfortunately, when classes went virtual due to COVID, many faced connectivity issues. I tried to support with Zoom, but ultimately, my own work had me logging unpredictable hours. We agreed it was best for me to step back, hoping to return in-person someday.

I started volunteering back in college around 2010 in Portland, where I worked with Portland Community Food Share from 2013 to 2016. I was involved in intake, helping families navigate their options with dignity, and even trained new volunteers on food safety. I had to leave when I moved to Seattle for my job at North Sound Data Solutions in August 2019. It's been a journey from Portland to Seattle, aligning my career with my passions.

Speaking of work, my current phone contact has changed to (206) 555-2843, which you can use to reach me outside of emails. Also, I’ve been reminiscing about those days at Bridgeview Research & Consulting in Portland, where I started in 2015. It was there that I honed my skills in data analysis, which set the stage for my current position.

On the personal front, Maya and I are contemplating what the future holds, maybe even starting a family. It’s a lot to consider with our workloads and Seattle’s high cost of living. We’re still figuring it out, but it’s exciting to think about.

Let’s chat soon—I’d love to hear more about your teaching adventures in Tacoma!

Take care,
Laura
